front: Play: Septimus Hodge tutors Thomasina Coverly at Sidley Park; defines 'carnal embrace' as 'the practice of throwing one's arms around a side of beef'
Play: coin lands on heads 92 consecutive times; Guildenstern theorizes 'un-, sub- or supernatural forces'; retells Hamlet from two minor courtiers' perspective
back: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern Are Dead (Tom Stoppard)

front: Play: Vladimir Lenin, Tristan Tzara, and James Joyce mingle with diplomat Henry Carr in 1917 Zurich; Tzara makes a poem from cut-up words of Shakespeare's Sonnet 18
Play: Hannah Jarvis discovers that the 'hermit of Sidley Park' was Septimus Hodge; Bernard Nightingale's theory about Lord Byron killing Ezra Chater is disproven—Chater died of a monkey bite
Play: critics Moon and Birdboot watch a murder mystery set at Muldoon Manor; Moon is killed when he discovers the body on stage is Higgs
Play: jealous playwright Henry leaves Charlotte for actress Annie; good writing compared to a cricket bat; refuses to ghostwrite for the imprisoned soldier Brodie
Play: Viennese Jewish Merz family across 1899–1955; Nazis seize property during the Anschluss; ends with Rosa saying 'Auschwitz' to a list of family names
Play: Player and Tragedians emerge from barrels on the ship; Alfred forced into a skirt for The Rape of the Sabine Women; Player stabbed with retractable knife, stands to applause
back: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern Are Dead (Tom Stoppard)

front: Play: A. E. Housman relives his unrequited love for Moses Jackson while crossing the River Styx to the afterlife
Play: trilogy (Voyage, Shipwreck, Salvage) about 19th-century Russian intellectuals including Alexander Herzen, Ivan Turgenev, and Mikhail Bakunin
Play: Thomasina Coverly uses rice pudding to illustrate the Second Law of Thermodynamics; models an early fractal; play ends with Thomasina and Septimus waltzing
Play: Act 1 spoken entirely in limericks; another scene parodies the catechism chapter of Ulysses; Tzara chants 'Dada' 34 times; Carr labels Wilde a 'Gommorahist'
Author: wrote Shakespeare in Love; wrote 'almost every dialogue' of Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade; co-wrote Brazil (creating 'Form 27B/6' alluding to Orwell's flat)
Author: Czech-born British playwright; born Tomas Straussler; shares his birth city (Prague) with Václav Havel; knighted as Sir Tom Stoppard
Play: 'Game of Questions' in which rhetoricals and repetitions are fouls; characters insert variations into 'Give us this day our daily bread'; death is 'the absence of presence, nothing more'
back: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern Are Dead (Tom Stoppard)

front: Play: features logical-positivist gymnasts; inspector Bones investigates McFee's murder; a character steps on his tortoise and accidentally shoots his hare while disproving Zeno's paradox
Play: opens with Hilary and Spike debating the 'hard problem of consciousness'; Hilary designs an experiment to prove human altruism; Amal's model fails to predict a stock market crash
Play: Annie cast in 'Tis Pity She's a Whore, begins an affair with co-star Billy; opens with a play-within-a-play (House of Cards) gradually revealed to be fictional
